description: Multiple admin/debug command handlers bypass the framework i18n layer: they emit hardcoded English strings with legacy §-section colour codes through raw sender.sendMessage(...) / sendRichMessage(...) instead of Message keys. The charter explicitly flags "hardcoded strings, legacy §/ChatColor codes, raw sender.sendMessage(...) in handlers". TreasuryCommand (reload, ingest, adminBalance, adminInfo, doAdminTransfer), PayAccountCommand.root, and AccountResolver all do this; every user-facing line in these paths is untranslatable and uses the deprecated legacy colour format rather than the MiniMessage/Message pipeline the rest of the plugin uses. TaxCommand is reported separately (seq 2) because it uses the distinct sendRichMessage API pervasively.
recommendation: Route every player-facing line through Message.send(sender, "treasury.<key>", ...) with the strings defined in messages.properties, matching PayCommand/BalanceCommand/GovCommand. Remove all §-codes; use MiniMessage tags in the message file. AccountResolver should either take a Message dependency or return a typed failure the caller renders via a Message key rather than messaging the sender itself.

occurrences.snippet:
sender.sendMessage("§e" + r.label() + " §7balance: §a" + accountService.formatAmount(balance));
...
        sender.sendMessage("§7archived=§f" + a.isArchived()
                + " §7requiresAuth=§f" + a.isRequiresAuthorization()

verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim at lines 81-84 of TreasuryCommand.java: sender.sendMessage("§cReload failed: "...) and sender.sendMessage("§aReloaded config.yml..."). These are hardcoded English strings with legacy § colour codes emitted via raw sender.sendMessage. The same pattern recurs in adminIngest (lines 124-139). The plugin clearly has an i18n pipeline (info() at line 92 uses message.send(sender, "treasury.info", ...)), so these admin/debug handlers genuinely bypass it, making them untranslatable and using the deprecated colour format. Minor severity is fair for admin-facing untranslatable output.

description: TaxCommand's /tax status and /tax trigger handlers build all player-facing output from hardcoded MiniMessage strings sent via raw sender.sendRichMessage(...) rather than Message keys. Roughly two dozen lines (headers, cycle rows, plugin lists, the trigger confirmation) are inlined in the handler, making them untranslatable and putting presentation logic in the command layer. The charter requires all player-facing text via Message keys and flags raw sender.send* in handlers.
recommendation: Move these strings into messages.properties as treasury.tax.status.* / treasury.tax.trigger.* keys and emit them via message.send(...). Where dynamic rows are assembled (formatCycleRow, formatNextFire), pass the computed values as placeholders to a keyed template instead of concatenating MiniMessage in Java.

description: The charter states resolver suggestions(...) runs off the main thread and must never touch live Bukkit state (Bukkit.getPlayer, world/entity access), being backed by service-managed caches instead. PayTargetResolver.suggestions iterates Bukkit.getOnlinePlayers() directly — live Bukkit state read from the suggestion thread on every keystroke. The government-name half of the suggestion is correctly served from a TTL cache; only the online-player enumeration reaches into live server state. Paper's online-player collection is a live mutable view, so an off-thread iteration can race concurrent join/quit mutation.
recommendation: Serve online-player suggestions from a service-managed cache updated on join/quit (or snapshot the roster on the main thread and hand the resolver an immutable copy), mirroring how governmentNames() is cached, so suggestions() touches no live Bukkit collection.

description: The membership/authorizer-based spend authorization for GOVERNMENT accounts lives entirely in the command layer (GovCommand.canTransferFrom / hasAuthorizerAccess / isAllowed and FineCommand.canFineFrom), not in the service. The charter says services must re-validate and enforce authorisation and not trust handlers. LedgerServiceImpl.transferInternal enforces only the account-level requires_authorization gate; a caller that reaches the service directly (or a future second caller) can move money out of any government account without the member/authorizer check, because that check is not part of the service contract. The single existing caller happens to guard correctly, so this is a layering/duplication risk rather than a live exploit.
recommendation: Move the per-account member/authorizer spend check into the service layer (e.g. a GovService/LedgerService method that takes the acting UUID and throws NoPermissionException), and have the handlers delegate to it, keeping the command layer to messaging only. This also removes the duplicated canTransferFrom/canFineFrom logic across GovCommand and FineCommand.

description: The charter calls for an interface + impl split for every service. Three service-package classes are concrete-only with no interface: BalanceTaxService (tax computation/collection, injects AccountMapper and is @Transactional), TaxCycleRegistry, and ConfigReloadService. Every other domain service (Account, Ledger, Gov, Membership, PlayerDirectory, etc.) follows the interface+impl split and is bound Interface->Impl in TreasuryModule; these three are bound as their concrete type, diverging from the established convention. BalanceTaxService in particular is a genuine domain service (it owns balance-tax business logic and calls a mapper), so the inconsistency is most notable there.
recommendation: Extract at least BalanceTaxService behind an interface (BalanceTaxService/BalanceTaxServiceImpl) bound in TreasuryModule, matching the rest of the service layer. TaxCycleRegistry (a mutable-state registry) and ConfigReloadService (a reload orchestrator) are borderline utilities; if they stay concrete, note that explicitly, but align BalanceTaxService with the convention.

snippet:
@EventHandler(priority = EventPriority.MONITOR, ignoreCancelled = true)
    public void onJoin(PlayerJoinEvent event) {
description: PlayerLoginListener handles PlayerJoinEvent at EventPriority.MONITOR while dispatching state-mutating work (recordLogin writes economy_players; balance tax collection writes the ledger). The charter flags MONITOR handlers that mutate state — MONITOR is conventionally reserved for pure observation of the final event outcome. Here the mutation is DB-side (not event/world state) and correctly hopped to an async task, so this is low-impact, but the priority choice is non-idiomatic for a handler whose purpose is to perform side-effecting persistence. FirstPlayerJoinEvent handles the same event at default priority, so the two join handlers also disagree on priority.
recommendation: Use the default (NORMAL) priority for a side-effecting join handler, reserving MONITOR for read-only observation; align both PlayerJoinEvent handlers on a deliberate, documented priority.
verification_note: Snippet matches verbatim at lines 54-55: @EventHandler(priority = EventPriority.MONITOR, ignoreCancelled = true) on onJoin(PlayerJoinEvent). The handler dispatches state-mutating work (recordLogin writes economy_players per lines 21-24/64; balanceTaxService.collect writes the ledger per line 67). MONITOR is conventionally for pure observation, so the priority is non-idiomatic for a side-effecting persistence handler. The finding correctly notes the mitigations (DB-side mutation, hopped to async task per lines 62-75) and rates it nit accordingly; severity is fair.
